draft.introduction: The Vafþrúðnismál (“Sayings of Vafþrúðnir”) is an Eddic wisdom contest between Odin and the giant Vafþrúðnir. The poem reveals cosmogonic lore about the world’s creation from the body of the primordial giant Ymir.

translation.text: From Ymir’s flesh the earth was shaped, from his blood the sea, mountains from his bones, trees from his hair, and from his skull the sky. From his brows the cheerful gods made Midgard for the sons of men, and from his brain were shaped the hard-tempered clouds.
translation.original_text: Ór Ymis holdi var jǫrð of skǪpuð, en ór beinum bjǪrg, himinn ór hausi ins hrímkalda jǫtuns, en ór sveita sjór.

translator_notes.note: The creation of the cosmos from a dismembered giant’s body is a widespread Indo-European motif, paralleled in the Vedic Purusha Sukta (RV 10.90) and the Iranian Gayomart tradition. The structural similarity suggests a common Proto-Indo-European cosmogonic myth.
